title: Some Basic Concept of Chemistry Test - 3 (Topic Test)

questions.questionText:
An unknown chlorohydrocarbon has 3.55% of chlorine. If each molecule of the hydrocarbon has one chlorine atom only; chlorine atoms present in 1 g of chlorohydrocarbon are :
(Atomic wt. of Cl = 35.5 u; Avogadro constant = 6.023 $$ \times $$ 1023 mol-1)
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.solution:
%    of   Cl  =  3.55

$$\therefore\,\,\,\,$$ In 100 g chlorohydrocarbon 3.55 gm Cl present.

In 1 gm chlorohydrocarbon Cl present

= $${{3.55} \over {100}}$$

= 0.0355 gm

$$\therefore\,\,\,\,$$ No of Moles of Cl = $${{0.0355} \over {35.5}}$$

= 0.001 mole

$$\therefore\,\,\,\,$$ no of Cl atoms = 0.001 $$ \times $$ 6.023 $$ \times $$ 103

= 6.023 $$ \times $$ 1020
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText:
The minimum amount of O2(g) consumed per gram of reactant is for the reaction :
(Given atomic mass : Fe = 56, O = 16, Mg = 24, P = 31, C = 12, H = 1)
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.solution:
(a) 4Fe(s) + 3O2(g) $$ \to $$ 2Fe2O3(s)

$${{Moles\,of\,{O_2}} \over 3} = {{Moles\,of\,{Fe}} \over 4}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ Moles of O2 = $${3 \over 4}$$ $$ \times $$ $${1 \over {56}}$$ moles = $${1 \over {24.8}}$$ moles = $${3 \over {224}} \times 32$$ g of O2 = 0.43 g of O2

(b) P4(s) + 5O2(g) $$ \to $$ P4O10(s)

$${{Moles\,of\,{O_2}} \over 5} = {{Moles\,of\,{P_4}} \over 1}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ Moles of O2 = 5 $$ \times $$ $${1 \over {124}}$$ moles = $${1 \over {24.8}}$$ moles = $${1 \over {24.8}} \times 32$$ g of O2 = 1.3 g of O2

(c) C3H8(g) + 5O2(g) $$ \to $$ 3CO2(g) + 4H2O(l)

$${{Moles\,of\,{O_2}} \over 5} = {{Moles\,of\,{C_3}{H_8}} \over 1}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ Moles of O2 = 5 $$ \times $$ $${1 \over {44}}$$ moles = $${1 \over {24.8}}$$ moles = $${1 \over {8.8}} \times 32$$ g of O2 = 3.6 g of O2

(d) 2Mg(s) + O2(g) $$ \to $$ 2MgO(s)

$${{Moles\,of\,{O_2}} \over 1} = {{Moles\,of\,{Mg}} \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ Moles of O2 = $${1 \over 2}$$ $$ \times $$ $${1 \over {24}}$$ moles = $${1 \over {48}}$$ moles = $${1 \over {48}} \times 32$$ g of O2 = 0.66 g of O2
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText:
At 300 K and 1 atmospheric pressure, 10 mL of a hydrocarbon required 55 mL of O<sub>2</sub> for complete
combustion, and 40 mL of CO<sub>2</sub> is formed. The formula of the hydrocarbon is :
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.solution:
CxHy + $$\left( {x + {y \over 4}} \right)$$O2 $$ \to $$ xCO2 + $${y \over 2}$$H2O

According to the above equation,

1 mL of hydrocarbon = x mL of CO2 is produced

$$ \therefore $$ 10 mL of hydrocarbon = 10x mL of CO2 is produced.

According to question,

From 10 mL of hydrocarbon 40 mL of CO2 is produced.

$$ \therefore $$ 10x = 40

$$ \Rightarrow $$ x = 4

Also from the above equation,

1 mL of CxHy react with $$\left( {x + {y \over 4}} \right)$$ mL of O2.

$$ \therefore $$ 10 mL of CxHy react with 10$$\left( {x + {y \over 4}} \right)$$ mL of O2.

According to question,

10$$\left( {x + {y \over 4}} \right)$$ = 55

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$40 + {{10y} \over 4} = 55$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ y = 6

$$ \therefore $$ Hydrocarbon is C4H6
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: The amount of sugar (C<sub>12</sub>H<sub>22</sub>O<sub>11</sub>) required to prepare 2L of its 0.1 M aqueous solution is :

questions.questionText: 25 ml of the given HCl solution requires 30 mL of 0.1 M sodium carbonate solution. What is the volume of this HCl solution required to titrate 30 mL of 0.2 M aqueous NaOH solutions ?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.solution:
2HCl(aq) + Na2CO3(aq) $$ \to $$ H2CO3 + NaCl

moles of HCl
2
 =
moles of Na2CO3
1

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${{M \times {{25} \over {1000}}} \over 2} = {{0.1 \times {{30} \over {1000}}} \over 1}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ Molarity of HCl (M) = $${6 \over {25}}M$$

HCl(aq) + NaOH(aq) $$ \to $$ NaCl + H2O

moles of HCl
1
 =
moles of NaOH
1

$${{{6 \over {25}} \times {V \over {1000}}} \over 1} = {{0.2 \times {{30} \over {1000}}} \over 1}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ V = 25 ml

questions.questionText: An organic compound is estimated through Duma's method and was found to evolve 6 moles of CO<sub>2</sub>. 4 moles of H<sub>2</sub>O and 1 mole of nitrogen gas. The formula of the compound is :
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.solution:
Molar ratio of C : H : N : : 6 : 8 : 2 i.e., 3 : 4 : 1

Thus, the correct formula is C6H8N2. .
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText:
A 10 mg effervescent tablet containing sodium bicarbonate and oxalic acid releases 0.25 ml of CO2 at T = 298.15 K and p = 1 bar. If molar volume of CO2 is 25.0 L under such condition, what is the percentage of sodium bicarbonate in each tablet ?
[Molar mass of NaHCO3 = 84 g mol–1]
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.solution:
2NaHCO3 + (COOH)2 $$ \to $$ (COONa)2 + 2H2O + 2CO2

$$ \therefore $$ 1 mole of CO2 is produced by 1 mole of NaHCO3.

Given, volume of CO2 produced = 0.25 ml

25 L of CO2 contains 1 mol

$$ \therefore $$ 0.25 ml of CO2 contains = $${1 \over {25 \times {{10}^3}}} \times 0.25$$ moles

= 10-5 moles

$$ \therefore $$ Moles of NaHCO3 = 10-5 moles

Mass of NaHCO3 = 10-5 $$ \times $$ 84 g

$$ \therefore $$ % of NaHCO3 in a tablet

= $${{84 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}} \over {10 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} \times 100$$ = 8.4 %
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText:
25 g of an unknown hydrocarbon upon burning produces 88 g of CO<sub>2</sub> and 9 g of H<sub>2</sub>O. This unknown
hydrocarbon contains :
options: 18 g of carbon and 7 g of hydrogen
20 g of carbon and 5 g of hydrogen
22 g of carbon and 3 g of hydrogen
24 g of carbon and 1 g of hydrogen
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.solution:
CxHy + $$\left( {x + {y \over 4}} \right)$$O2 $$ \to $$ xCO2 + $${y \over 2}$$H2O

No of moles of CO2 produced = $${{88} \over {44}}$$ = 2 mol
No of moles of H2O produced = $${9 \over {18}}$$ = 0.5 mol

According to stoichiometric mole mole analysis :

$${{moles\,of\,C{O_2}} \over x} = {{moles\,of\,{H_2}O} \over {{y \over 2}}}$$
$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${2 \over x} = {{0.5} \over {{y \over 2}}}$$
$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${x \over y} = {2 \over 1}$$

So, $${{no\,of\,atoms\,of\,'C'\,in\,{C_x}{H_y}} \over {no\,of\,atoms\,of\,'H'\,in\,{C_x}{H_y}}} = {2 \over 1}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${{Mass\,of\,'C'\,in\,{C_x}{H_y}} \over {Mass\,of\,'H'\,in\,{C_x}{H_y}}} = {{2 \times 12} \over {1 \times 1}} = {{24} \over 1}$$
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText:
Thermal decomposition of a Mn compound (X) at 513 K results in compound Y, MnO<sub>2</sub> and gaseous product.
MnO<sub>2</sub> reacts with NaCl and concentrated H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>4</sub> to give a pungent gas Z. X, Y and Z, respectively, are :
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.solution:
KMnO4 $$\buildrel {513\,\,K} \over \longrightarrow $$ K2MnO4 + MnO2 + O2
(X) (Y)
MnO2 + NaCl + conc H2SO4 $$ \to $$ MnSO4 + NaHSO4 + H2O + Cl2
(Z)
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText:
5 moles of AB<sub>2</sub> weigh 125 × 10<sup>–3</sup>
 kg and 10 moles of A<sub>2</sub>B<sub>2</sub> weigh 300 × 10<sup>–3</sup>
 kg. The molar mass of A (M<sub>A</sub>)
and molar mass of B (M<sub>B</sub>) in kg mol are:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.solution:
Molar weigh of 'A' = MA

Molar weigh of 'B' = MB

Molar mass of AB2 = MA + 2MB

Molar mass of A2B2 = 2(MA + MB)

5 mol AB2 weighs 125 g

$$ \therefore $$ AB2 = 25 g/mol

10 mol A2B2 weighs 300 g

$$ \therefore $$ A2B2 = 30 g/mol

So, MA + 2MB = 25 ..............(1)

and 2(MA + MB) = 30 ..............(2)

By solving (1) and (2),

MA = 5 gm = 5 $$ \times $$ 10-3 kg

MB = 10 gm = 10 $$ \times $$ 10-3 kg
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.solution:
We know,

molality(m) = $${{{x_{solute}}} \over {{x_{solvent}}}} \times {{1000} \over {molar\,weight\,of\,solvent}}$$

Here solvent is H2O

xsolvent = 0.8

$$ \therefore $$ xsolute = 0.2

$$ \therefore $$ Molality = $${{0.2} \over {0.8}} \times {{1000} \over {18}}$$ = 13.88
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: 8 g of NaOH is dissolved in 18g of H<sub>2</sub>O. Mole fraction of NaOH in solution and molality (in mol kg<sup>–1</sup>) of the solution respectively are -
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.solution:
8 gm of NaOH   =   $${8 \over {40}}$$   =   0.2 mol of NaOH

18 gm of H2O   =   $${18 \over {18}}$$   =   1 mol of H2O

$$ \therefore $$  Total mole   =   1 + 0.2   =   1.2 mol

$$ \therefore $$  Mole fraction of NaOH   =   $${{0.2} \over {1.2}}$$   =   0.167

We know,

Molality   =   $${{Moles\,\,of\,\,solute} \over {Weight\,of\,solvent}} \times $$ 1000

     =  $${{0.2} \over {18}} \times $$ 1000

     =  11.11
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText:
The percentage composition of carbon by mole
in methane is :
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.solution:
In CH4, there are 5 atoms (1C + 4H).

$$ \therefore $$ % composition of C by mole in CH4

= $${1 \over 5}$$ $$ \times $$ 100 = 20 %
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText:
What would be the molality of 20% (mass/ mass) aqueous solution of KI?
(molar mass of KI = 166 g mol–1)
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.solution:
20% (mass/ mass) aqueous solution means,

100 g solution contains 20 g KI

$$ \therefore $$ Mass of solvent = 100 – 20 = 80 g

So, 20 g of KI present in 80 g of solvent.

$$ \therefore $$ $${{20} \over {166}}$$ moles of KI present in 80 g of solvent.

We know,

Molality (m) = $${{no\,of\,moles\,of\,solute} \over {wt(in\,kg)\,of\,solvent}}$$

$$ \therefore $$ m = $${{{{20} \over {166}}} \over {{{80} \over {1000}}}}$$ = 1.51
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText:
For a reaction,
N2(g) + 3H2(g) $$ \to $$ 2NH3(g) ;
identify dihydrogen (H2) as a limiting reagent in the following reaction mixtures.
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.solution:
Here you have to check every options to find answer.

N2(g) + 3H2(g) $$ \to $$ 2NH3(g)

For dihydrogen (H2) to become a limiting reagent,

$${{moles\,of\,{H_2}} \over {stoichiometric\,coefficient\,of\,{H_2}}}$$ should be

less than $${{moles\,of\,{N_2}} \over {stoichiometric\,coefficient\,of\,{N_2}}}$$.

Here in 56g of N2 + 10g of H2,

Moles of N2 = $${{56} \over {28}}$$ = 2

and Moles of H2 = $${{10} \over 2}$$ = 5

Now by dividing stoichiometric coefficient we get,

For N2 = $${2 \over 1}$$ = 2

For H2 = $${5 \over 3}$$ = 1.67

$$ \therefore $$ Here H2 is the limiting reagent.
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.
